title: Class Creation and Instantiation

overview.what: A class is a blueprint for creating objects, defining their structure and behavior. When you instantiate a class, you create a unique object (instance) in memory based on that blueprint. Think of a class as a cookie cutter, and the objects as the cookies themselves. In Python, variables are labels, and objects are boxes; instantiation creates a new box, and `self` inside `__init__` is the label pointing to that newly created box.
overview.why: Classes allow you to group related data and functions together into a single, cohesive unit, making complex code organized and reusable.

why_learning_this: Grouping state and behavior for complex applications
Understanding how third-party libraries structure their APIs

common_mistakes.title: Forgetting self in __init__
common_mistakes.explanation: Python automatically passes the instance as the first argument to instance methods. Forgetting `self` causes a TypeError when creating the object.

common_mistakes.title: Modifying class attributes instead of instance attributes
common_mistakes.explanation: Defining variables outside `__init__` makes them class-level, shared across all instances. Always initialize instance-specific data inside `__init__`.

recall_questions.q: What is the difference between a class and an instance?
recall_questions.answer: A class is the blueprint (the cookie cutter), while an instance is the specific object created from that blueprint (the cookie).

recall_questions.q: What does instantiation do in memory?
recall_questions.answer: It creates a new unique object in memory based on the class blueprint.

practice_tasks.title: Modify User Class Initialization
practice_tasks.prompt: The `User` class currently only accepts and stores a `username`. Modify the `__init__` method so it also accepts an `email` parameter and saves it as an instance attribute. Then, update the instantiation call to pass an email.
practice_tasks.solution:
class User:
  def __init__(self, username, email):
    self.username = username
    self.email = email

user = User('alice', 'alice@example.com')
print(user.username)
print(user.email)

aha_moment.code:
class Config:
  tags = []

c1 = Config()
c2 = Config()
c1.tags.append('prod')
print(c2.tags)
aha_moment.why: Variables defined directly in the class body are class attributes, shared by all instances. To give each instance its own list, initialize it inside `__init__` using `self.tags = []`.

challenge.title: Database Connection Simulation
challenge.prompt: Create a `DatabaseConnection` class. The `__init__` method should take a `database_url`. It should also initialize an `is_connected` attribute to `False`. Then, instantiate the class with `'postgresql://localhost/mydb'` and print the `is_connected` attribute.

understanding_checks.question: What will this code print?
understanding_checks.why: Each time a class is instantiated, a new unique object (box) is created in memory. `s1` and `s2` are labels pointing to different boxes, each with its own `port` value.

understanding_checks.question: A developer thinks that calling `Server(8000)` merely modifies a global `Server` object. What's wrong with this mental model?
understanding_checks.answer: The class is a blueprint (cookie cutter), not the object itself. Instantiation creates a brand new unique object (cookie) in memory every time it's called.
understanding_checks.why: Understanding that classes are blueprints and instantiation allocates new memory is crucial to understanding object-oriented programming in Python.
